title: I Hired Claude Code as My Employee

content.text: You're at lunch. You pull out your phone
and tell Claude to build a landing page
for a new product launch. And by the
time you're back at your desk, it's
done. This is possible because last
week, Anthropic shipped co-work
dispatch. But this now works with Claude
Code, too. So, you can fully control
Claude Code from your phone. We took
this a step further and built a system
where we can assign work to Claude Code
directly from Notion. That changes
Notion from being our task manager to
our task executor. It literally does all
the work itself. Now, we'll also show
you Claude's new computer use feature
where it takes over your keyboard and
mouse and a skill we built that oneshots
entire websites. I'm joined by Gail
Breton, my co-founder, Authority Hacker,
where we separate what works for
business owners and knowledge workers
from what's just noise in the AI space.
How's it going, Gail?
>> Like the new catchphrase at the
beginning. It's like, [laughter]
did AI write this?
>> It did, but I kind of directed it. I do
in this space.
>> It sounds like a local news station's uh
catchphrase a little bit, which is like
it's both good and bad, you know? But
>> there's maybe a reason why all news
stations have things like this. And one
of the problems we had with our podcast
is like new people come and they're
like, who is this for? Who are you guys?
What is this? It's fine if you've been
listening for 10 years, but not everyone
has. So for the benefit of those people,
we hope you're in the right place now.
And we got a bunch of new listeners last
week actually. So, welcome to everyone.
>> Yeah, thanks for tuning in this week as
well if you just found us last week.
Today we're going to talk about
controlling Claude remotely in various
ways, some very basic and some a bit
more advanced.
>> So, Gail, let's start off if we can with
co-work. It was originally called
co-work dispatch, but my understanding
is that it now works with claude code.
So, it's just yeah, just dispatch.
>> Um, yeah, I've told a lot of people
about this. this I was at an event last
week and the first question everyone
said was oh can it control my VS code or
how is it working and it can't but it
kind of can do the same thing anyway so
you want to kind of fill us in on
exactly what's going on here I mean
there's two things right there's the
dispatch that connects cloud code but it
connects to cloud code on your desktop
app and it's kind of a weird one right
because when you use dispatch you
basically have this one feed with
everything and it just never clears it
it's just one feed and then that acts as
an orchestrator that then starts either
co-work sessions or code code sessions
on your desktop app which with whatever
setting you have on your desktop app. So
if you haven't connected your MCPS on
your desktop app or whatever it's not
going to work but if you have it will
work actually and it kind of decides
what to do like it's you don't really
have too much control.
>> You can replicate VS Code essentially by
setting the folder it's working on to be
the same folder that your VS Code works
on
>> enough but not really. So let's imagine
for example we have this meta ad scale
that we showed a few episodes ago that
creates images right to run that skill
you need a Gemini API key because it
uses an open banana right it's like it's
not a skill it's like it's just an API
key that it calls when it calls the API
and as a result it's like you cannot
store secrets in the cloud app for
example right now so you could not run
that skill for example it wouldn't work
so it's like there are still limitations
and it's still not perfect like it's
evolving like you know by this time next
week it will probably be solved given
and how fast these things are being
done, etc. And it's kind of like it
works very well if you have your code on
GitHub, but then there's still like the
secret management that's still a little
bit janky. Like you can make
environments, but it's not very clear.
Like entropic is throwing a lot of [ __ ]
at the wall right now. A lot of it kind
of overlaps and it's like 80% done every
time they release it. So it's like it's
kind of good, but it's kind of also not
that good because it's all vibe coded.
And so like my guess is they're just
throwing everything they can at the
wall. They see what people use and they
consolidate. I can imagine that
eventually they will focus on the things
that people use and and abandon the
things that people don't use and
depreciate them.
>> So right now, how are you using it or
how would a business owner be using
this? Yeah,
>> I'm not using it because I built a
better system on notion that we're talk
about later.
>> Okay.
>> Um so I am
>> that aside like what would you be using
it for?
>> I don't think I would use it for cl
code. I think the best way to use it
right now is to actually focus on
co-work connect your MCPs connect it to
your folders and then have skill loaded
on co-work and then you can run all
these stuff. It's pretty good on
co-work. It will not do everything you
can do on VS Code if you're on VS Code,
but it can do, you know, 70% of it. Uh
it doesn't have a terminal, but that's
how I would set it up. And so I've set
it up a little bit with co-work. It
works kind of okay, but I really don't
like the way it works because it's
basically like it works like a chat, but
it's not really like a chat. It's an
orchestrator. You talk to the
orchestrator and then it just spawns a
session of co-work, but you see nothing.
So, you're on your phone and it's just
kind of like idle for 10 minutes while
it's running the co-work session and
it's like boom, it's done or it failed
or it just kind of like hangs because
it's like it's failed silently or
something. What's good is it does have
the approval thing. So if it needs
access to a folder or something, it will
just pop you like I think you can see it
on the Twitter image here. Like it asks
you for access and so that's decent. It
works, but it still feels like you don't
really know. It's kind of this weird
hybrid where there's a chat happening in
the background where you don't see the
chat. So you don't see what it's doing.
So you don't know if it's going well or
not well. You cannot interrupt like load
code. And so that's why it's like bro
type thing.
>> Well, it's not just that. It's like I
want to read this file and you're like
why? It's like why are you doing this?
And it's like you don't know. Um, and so
like that's why actually the notion
system I built, I like it better because
it's fully asynchronous. So you kind of
assume it's just going to go do its
task, but you set it as a task, it just
comes back to it, but you're not like
hanging on the chat. It's just doing it
basically.
>> Ju just before we jump on the notion
thing because I can see you're eager to
get there.
>> Not that it's just like I don't use this
dispatch. Basically,
>> one of the things that I hate a lot
about the AI industry is the weak use
cases. you know, they'll they'll release
this new feature and like,
>> yeah, you can,
>> you know, use your voice to send an
email or like, you know, look up the
weather or you open your calculator or
something like and it's like that's
stupid. That's a stupid use case. Are
there any realistic practical use cases
that business owners or knowledge
workers would use dispatch for today?
>> Yeah. If you have any MCPS that has
valuable data, let's say you again lots
of SEOs listening to us, right? like
you're on a meeting with a client and
you need to pull the latest data from
their site and you have a small phone
like I have. It's kind of complicated.
You could open HS but honestly like
navigating HS with all the filters
sounds like a nightmare. If you have the
HS MCP connected to coark you can just

the whole thing for you and it can make

on the go or you're in the car or
whatever and you can use your tools that
are connected via MCP or skill that
don't require API keys, just MCPS. Um,
so still pretty good.
>> I'm thinking as well if you're at like a
conference or an event and you're
speaking to someone, you could, you
know, like pull it up there and then and
in a couple minutes like during the
conversation, you could have like
something meaningful to show them too.
>> With the new code integration, like you
know, we're going to show the site
building skill, right? I've built this
skill to like start new sites. Um, you
could literally talk to someone and just
prompt on your phone. I would start a

up prototypes like and then as you're
having that conversation is building
like I do that quite often on calls. I
just launch these things and I'm like I
know that's what they want to do and I
show them I can achieve 80% of it by the
end of the call. So you you could do
that like a site redesign or a landing
page concept or like a marketing
campaign you talked about be like hey
you can prototype this, you can do all
of that. Like it can do all of that. So
it's good. So
>> for people who are meeting others,
meeting clients, meeting prospects, like
in the field face to face, you know,
going to their office for a meeting or
an event or a conference, then there's
probably quite a lot of use cases here.
But for you and I who like are scared to
leave our bedrooms, um, you know, maybe
not quite so much.
>> Uh, yeah. I mean I think it's really the
idea of like you can take a lot of
clothes capabilities outside of like you
know having a big screen having multiple

for you and it's like it's a first
attempt at it. I personally like the

where it's like you know you don't

getting there and that's what's
happening. People are kind of like
working more with these tools right now
more hours and they kind of feel like oh
my god I have all these limits left. I
need to use it right now or like I could
just keep it going. It's not super
difficult. I need to go do groceries
where I kind of want to keep my thread
going etc. You can start doing these
things now as this is going. This is
also a big dig at open obviously like
this is basically how open CL was

there's not really much use case for

>> But you can there's plenty of tools to

uh yeah, it's it's not too bad. It's
just yeah in the use case when like
you're traveling your laptop is in your

everything then it's not going to work.
Yeah.
>> So you weren't a huge fan of open claw

>> so yeah it's better for 99% of people.
It's probably like a bit worse for some

a point if you haven't picked it up and
if you don't have your whole setup yet
on open I don't think there's a point
doing it anymore. You might as well just
start on cloud and in two weeks it will
cover all use cases.

That's it. And then if I set it to to-do

workflow. Um, and the point is like,
yeah, I just basically
